ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn provides a general chicken soup class; Malay-specific spiced variant not separately coded. Using closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP maps to chicken soup as a prepared dish; Malay-spiced variant not separately enumerated.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Malay chicken soup; general food substance codes not sufficiently specific to warrant assignment.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sup Ayam is approximately one bowl of 350 ml (350 g), typically containing chicken pieces, potatoes, and carrots in a clear spiced broth.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken soup, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD chicken soup reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for home-prepared chicken soup (FDC ID 172152) and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD values for Malay-style chicken soup. Values reflect a clear broth preparation with chicken, root vegetables, and whole spices. Input enriched_json values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with these references and have been retained where they align. Saturated fat estimated from USDA chicken soup fatty acid profile.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Soups — analogous estimate based on chicken soup with vegetables; University of Sydney GI Database reference for vegetable-chicken broth soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Malay-style Sup Ayam found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy to clear chicken and vegetable soups listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 30–40 for similar preparations. The high protein and liquid content, combined with moderate carbohydrate from potatoes and carrots, supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.75) / 100 ≈ 5.5 g (carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 4.5 × 3.5 = 15.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 220 mg per 100 g; a 350 ml serving delivers approximately 770 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit portion size to one small bowl and avoid adding extra salt or MSG, as the sodium content of a full serving approaches one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and moderate carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) with high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sup Ayam is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index and high protein content; however, portion control for potato content is advised for those on strict carbohydrate-counting regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (2.5 g per 100 g) and low saturated fat (estimated 0.7 g per 100 g); anti-inflammatory spices present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low fat and saturated fat profile of Sup Ayam makes it a heart-friendly choice; ginger and garlic contribute anti-inflammatory compounds, though sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from ginger and garlic
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and garlic in Sup Ayam provide bioactive anti-inflammatory compounds that may offer modest benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sup Ayam is a classic Malay clear chicken soup widely consumed across Malaysia and Singapore. It is prepared by simmering bone-in or boneless chicken pieces with aromatic whole spices including star anise, cinnamon sticks, and cloves, together with ginger, garlic, and shallots. Root vegetables such as potatoes and carrots are added to the broth, which is cooked until the chicken is tender and the flavours are well-integrated. The soup is finished with fried shallots and fresh Chinese celery (daun sup) or spring onions as garnish. The resulting dish has a clear, golden-amber broth with a mildly spiced, savoury, and slightly sweet flavour profile. The texture is light and comforting, with tender chicken and soft vegetables. Nutritionally, Sup Ayam is a low-calorie, low-fat, moderate-protein dish providing approximately 65 kcal per 100 g, with 6.2 g protein and 2.5 g fat. It is a good source of niacin (3.2 mg), vitamin A (85 mcg RAE), potassium (180 mg), and phosphorus (85 mg) per 100 g. The sodium content of 220 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals on sodium-restricted diets. Its estimated low glycemic index of 35 makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served with steamed white rice or as a broth base for noodle soups, and is appropriate for breakfast, lunch, or dinner. It is particularly valued as a restorative meal during illness or recovery.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Malay chicken soup?
Is Sup Ayam good for diabetics?
Is Sup Ayam high in sodium?
What spices are in Sup Ayam?
Can I eat Sup Ayam if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of Sup Ayam?
What vegetables are in Sup Ayam?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken soup reference and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. The input enriched_json values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are consistent with approved-source ranges and have been retained. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA fatty acid profile for chicken soup. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar clear chicken-vegetable soups. No exact Sup Ayam entry was found in a single approved primary source, hence medium confidence.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for Malay-style Sup Ayam are not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-16. All core macros (protein_g 6.2, fat_g 2.5, carbohydrate_g 4.5) are non-zero and consistent with the dish composition; no zero-macro justification is required. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sup Ayam was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving (350 g) calculated as (35 × 15.75) / 100 ≈ 5.5 g. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 (chicken soup) and FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken soup) used as closest defensible matches; Malay-spiced variant not separately coded in either system.
